labels.name: Endpoint
labels.desc: A URL path the API exposes to the outside world

labels.name: Request
labels.desc: Data arriving from the client — path variables, params, or body

labels.name: Response
labels.desc: What the API sends back to the client

labels.name: Service layer
labels.desc: Where business logic lives — between the controller and data access

labels.name: Repository
labels.desc: The layer responsible for reading from and writing to the database

labels.name: Entity
labels.desc: A Java class that maps directly to a database table

labels.name: Annotation
labels.desc: Metadata that tells Spring how to wire or handle something

labels.name: Injection
labels.desc: Spring automatically providing a class with the dependencies it needs

prompts.title: Products Endpoint
prompts.statement: An e-commerce API needs to serve all products. Callers should also be able to narrow the results by category without a separate endpoint. The business logic for filtering should sit between the API surface and the data layer, and that layer should be injected into the controller automatically.

expectedTags.phrase: serve all products
expectedTags.explanation: Serving products means exposing a URL the outside world can call (endpoint) and reading records from the database (repository).

expectedTags.phrase: narrow the results by category
expectedTags.explanation: The category filter is data coming in from the caller — that makes it part of the request.

expectedTags.phrase: sit between the API surface and the data layer
expectedTags.explanation: The service layer is precisely this middle tier — it holds business logic and sits between the controller (API surface) and the repository (data layer).

expectedTags.phrase: injected into the controller automatically
expectedTags.explanation: Spring's dependency injection means the framework provides the service to the controller without the controller constructing it manually.

prompts.title: Create Order Endpoint
prompts.statement: An API endpoint accepts a new order from a client. Before saving it, the server should confirm the order contains at least one item. The API should notify the client whether the order was created.

expectedTags.phrase: An API endpoint accepts a new order from a client
expectedTags.explanation: This describes both the URL the API exposes (endpoint) and the data arriving from the caller — the order itself (request).

expectedTags.phrase: confirm the order contains at least one item
expectedTags.explanation: Validation logic — deciding whether an order is acceptable before persisting it — belongs in the service layer, not the controller.

expectedTags.phrase: notify the client whether the order was created
expectedTags.explanation: Whatever the API sends back to tell the caller about the outcome of their request is the response.

prompts.title: Product by ID
prompts.statement: Clients need to look up a single product by its ID. If the ID doesn't exist in the system, the API should respond with a meaningful not-found status.

expectedTags.phrase: look up a single product by its ID
expectedTags.explanation: Exposing a lookup by ID means defining a URL for that purpose (endpoint) and treating the ID as data arriving from the caller (request).

expectedTags.phrase: If the ID doesn't exist in the system
expectedTags.explanation: Checking whether a record exists requires querying the database — that is the repository's responsibility.

expectedTags.phrase: respond with a meaningful not-found status
expectedTags.explanation: The status code and body the API sends back to the caller — whether a product or a not-found indicator — is the response.

prompts.title: Update Customer Email
prompts.statement: A customer wants to update the email address on their account. The API should accept the customer's ID and the new email address and return the updated customer record.

expectedTags.phrase: update the email address on their account
expectedTags.explanation: Applying the change is business logic that belongs in the service layer, and the account being modified is a database-backed entity.

expectedTags.phrase: accept the customer's ID and the new email address
expectedTags.explanation: Accepting these values means the API exposes a URL for this operation (endpoint) and the ID and email are data sent in by the caller (request).

expectedTags.phrase: return the updated customer record
expectedTags.explanation: Sending the changed record back to the caller is the response — it confirms what was saved.

prompts.title: Discount Code Service
prompts.statement: An order total calculation involves applying a discount code before returning the final amount. That logic should live in a dedicated layer, separate from the part of the system that handles incoming requests. The dedicated layer should be wired up automatically and should read discount records from the database.

expectedTags.phrase: dedicated layer
expectedTags.explanation: The service layer is that dedicated tier for business logic, and an annotation is what tells Spring to recognize and manage it as such.

expectedTags.phrase: wired up automatically
expectedTags.explanation: Automatic wiring is dependency injection — Spring provides the layer with everything it needs without manual construction.

expectedTags.phrase: handles incoming requests
expectedTags.explanation: The part of the system that receives calls from outside is the controller, which sits at the endpoint layer.

expectedTags.phrase: read discount records from the database
expectedTags.explanation: Reading from the database is the repository's job, and the discount records being read are entities — Java classes mapped to database tables.

prompts.title: Paginated User List
prompts.statement: An admin dashboard needs to browse users in pages rather than loading everyone at once. The endpoint should accept the page number and page size as optional query parameters.

expectedTags.phrase: endpoint should accept the page number and page size as optional query parameters
expectedTags.explanation: This names the URL the API exposes (endpoint) and identifies the pagination values as caller-supplied input (request).

expectedTags.phrase: browse users in pages rather than loading everyone at once
expectedTags.explanation: Deciding how to page through results is business logic in the service layer, which then delegates the actual database read to the repository.

prompts.title: Request Logger
prompts.statement: The operations team wants every incoming API request logged with its method and how long it took to produce a response. This behavior should apply to all requests automatically, without touching each controller.

expectedTags.phrase: every incoming API request
expectedTags.explanation: An incoming API request is data arriving from a caller (request) at one of the URLs the system exposes (endpoint).

expectedTags.phrase: how long it took to produce a response
expectedTags.explanation: Measuring response time means tracking the moment the API finishes and sends data back to the caller — that outbound data is the response.

expectedTags.phrase: apply to all requests automatically, without touching each controller
expectedTags.explanation: An annotation tells Spring to register the filter as a managed component, and injection means Spring wires it into the request pipeline without any controller knowing about it.

prompts.title: Delete Product Endpoint
prompts.statement: An admin endpoint lets authorized users remove a product by ID. When a product is deleted, all associated order line items should also be removed. The response should confirm the deletion.

expectedTags.phrase: admin endpoint lets authorized users remove a product by ID
expectedTags.explanation: The URL exposed for this delete operation is the endpoint, and the product ID supplied by the caller is the request data.

expectedTags.phrase: all associated order line items should also be removed
expectedTags.explanation: Order line items are entities — records mapped to a database table — and removing them requires the repository to perform the delete against the database.

expectedTags.phrase: The response should confirm the deletion
expectedTags.explanation: Sending confirmation back to the caller is the response — it closes the loop so the client knows the operation succeeded.
